claim.content: The variationally computed quasiparticle weight $g_{t}=8(1-2d)d$ is strongly reduced as the first-order Mott transition is approached from the metallic side; identifying $g_{t}$ with the quasiparticle residue $Z$ implies a low-energy quasiparticle effective mass scaling $m^{*}/m\sim 1/g_{t}$ (where $m$ is the noninteracting band mass), so that the reductions of $g_{t}$ near $U_{c}$ correspond to mass-enhancement factors of order three to four, and to concomitant reductions in the Drude weight and in the superfluid stiffness in the variational mean-field description.

conclusion.content: The variationally computed quasiparticle weight $g_{t}=8(1-2d)d$ is strongly reduced as the first-order Mott transition is approached from the metallic side; identifying $g_{t}$ with the quasiparticle residue $Z$ implies a low-energy quasiparticle effective mass scaling $m^{*}/m\sim 1/g_{t}$ (where $m$ is the noninteracting band mass), so that the reductions of $g_{t}$ near $U_{c}$ correspond to mass-enhancement factors of order three to four, and to concomitant reductions in the Drude weight and in the superfluid stiffness in the variational mean-field description.

premises.content: Let $g_{t}$ be the Gutzwiller renormalization factor given by $g_{t}=8(1-2d)d$, let $Z$ denote the quasiparticle residue (the weight of the coherent single-particle pole in the retarded Green's function), and let $m^{*}$ be the low-energy quasiparticle effective mass defined from the dispersion of the coherent quasiparticle band. Within the Gutzwiller approximation combined with a Hartree-Fock-Gorkov mean-field treatment of the Hubbard(-Heisenberg) model at half-filling on the anisotropic triangular lattice, $g_{t}$ is identified with $Z$, and the low-energy effective mass scales parametrically as $m^{*}/m\approx 1/g_{t}$ (with $m$ the noninteracting band mass), so that reductions of $g_{t}$ map to enhancements of $m^{*}$ and to reductions of Drude weight and superfluid stiffness in the proportional sense used to estimate numeric enhancement factors.

Using the counting-argument relation $g_{t}=8(1-2d)d$ and the identification $m^{*}/m\approx1/g_{t}$, the numerical values of $g_{t}$ obtained in the variational calculation near the computed critical interaction $U_{c}$ imply effective-mass enhancements $m^{*}/m$ of order $3$–$4$ for the coherent quasiparticle excitations in the half-filled layered organic superconductor parameter regime considered, with the understanding that this estimate neglects additional many-body renormalizations beyond the Gutzwiller and Hartree-Fock-Gorkov approximations.

steps.reasoning: Take as established the upstream result that the quasiparticle renormalization factor $g_t$ is given by $g_t=8(1-2d)d$ and plays the role of the quasiparticle weight $Z$ which reduces band-width and Drude weight and enhances effective mass $m^*$.

Describe the numerical behavior of $g_t$ approaching the Mott transition: the computed $g_t(U)$ curves (obtained by solving the mean-field/RVB equations and minimizing $d$) show that $g_t$ is strongly reduced as the Mott transition is approached from the metallic side, with $g_t$ dropping towards zero at the first-order transition point $U_c$.
Fig. 2

Connect the reduction in $g_t$ to an effective mass enhancement using the relation between quasiparticle weight and mass: the effective mass renormalization is given parametrically by $m^*/m\sim1/g_t$ (i.e., smaller $g_t$ implies larger $m^*$), so a strong reduction of $g_t$ implies a commensurate enhancement of $m^*$.

Give the quantitative estimate reported by the authors for the mass enhancement near the transition: from the magnitude of the computed $g_t$ near $U_c$ the authors infer an effective mass enhancement by factors of about 3–4 near the Mott transition.
Fig. 2

State the concomitant effects on transport and superconducting properties: because $g_t$ reduces the Drude weight and the superfluid stiffness (the superfluid stiffness scales with the quasiparticle weight in the simplest interpretation), the strong suppression of $g_t$ near the Mott transition corresponds to a suppression of the Drude weight and of the superfluid stiffness.

Note the comparison to experiment made by the authors: these quantitative trends in $g_t(U)$ and the inferred mass enhancement and reduced superfluid stiffness are stated to be consistent with the large effective masses and reduced superfluid stiffness observed experimentally in half-filled layered organic superconductors, with the authors citing experimental/theoretical literature supporting this comparison.
[7]

motivating_questions.content: Layered organic superconductors close to the Mott insulating state display large enhancements of the quasiparticle effective mass and a superfluid stiffness much smaller than predicted by weak-coupling BCS theory. Weak-coupling and single-particle approximations do not capture the many-body renormalization of the low-energy spectral weight and hence cannot explain the magnitude of these experimental anomalies. A microscopic computation that yields an explicit quasiparticle renormalization factor as a function of $U$ and lattice anisotropy is required to show that strong correlation effects inherent in the minimal model can quantitatively account for the observed mass enhancement and suppressed stiffness.
